# Mary I of England (1516–1558)

## Overview

Mary I (18 February 1516 – 17 November 1558) was Queen of England and Ireland from July 1553 until her death in 1558. She was also Queen of Spain as the wife of King Philip II from January 1556. She was the first queen regnant of England — that is, the first woman to rule in her own right — and she is best known for her vigorous but ultimately unsuccessful attempts to reverse the English Reformation and restore Roman Catholicism. During her five-year reign, more than 280 religious dissenters were burned at the stake, earning her the posthumous nickname **"Bloody Mary."** [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England]

---

## Birth and Family

Mary was born on **18 February 1516** at the **Palace of Placentia** in Greenwich, England. She was the only surviving child of **King Henry VIII** and his first wife, **Catherine of Aragon**. Before Mary's birth, Catherine had endured multiple miscarriages, stillbirths, and one short-lived son, Henry, Duke of Cornwall. Mary was baptized into the Catholic faith three days after her birth at the Church of the Observant Friars in Greenwich. Her godparents included Lord Chancellor Thomas Wolsey and her great-aunt Catherine, Countess of Devon. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Birth and family]

---

## Childhood and Education

Mary was a **precocious child**. By July 1520, when she was barely four and a half years old, she entertained a visiting French delegation with a performance on the virginals (a type of harpsichord). Much of her early education came from her mother, Catherine of Aragon, who consulted the Spanish humanist **Juan Luis Vives** and commissioned him to write *De Institutione Feminae Christianae*, a treatise on the education of girls. By age nine, Mary could read and write Latin, and she also studied French, Spanish, music, dance, and possibly Greek. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Childhood]

Henry VIII doted on Mary and boasted to the Venetian ambassador that she never cried. She had a **fair complexion with pale blue eyes and red or reddish-golden hair**, resembling both her parents, and was ruddy-cheeked like her father. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Childhood]

Despite his affection, Henry was deeply disappointed at having no male heir. In 1525, he sent nine-year-old Mary to the Welsh Marches to preside over the Council of Wales and the Marches, based at **Ludlow Castle**, granting her many of the royal prerogatives normally reserved for a Prince of Wales — though she was never formally invested with that title. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Childhood]

### Early Marriage Negotiations

Throughout her childhood, Henry negotiated various potential marriages for Mary:
- **Age 2**: Promised to Francis, Dauphin of France (infant son of King Francis I); the contract was repudiated after three years.
- **Age 6 (1522)**: Contracted to marry her 22-year-old cousin **Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor**; the engagement was broken off within a few years.
- Negotiations followed with France (to marry either King Francis I or his second son), and later with Scotland (her cousin James V). None came to fruition. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Childhood]

---

## Adolescence and the King's "Great Matter"

Mary's comfortable world collapsed when her father sought to annul his marriage to Catherine of Aragon. Henry claimed the marriage was unclean because Catherine had been the widow of his brother Arthur, citing Leviticus 20:21. Catherine insisted her first marriage had never been consummated. **Pope Clement VII** refused to grant the annulment, influenced partly by the fact that Catherine's nephew Charles V had sacked Rome in 1527. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Adolescence]

From 1531, Mary suffered from **irregular menstruation and depression**, though it remains unclear whether this was caused by stress, puberty, or an underlying disease. She was not permitted to see her mother, whom Henry had sent away from court. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Adolescence]

In early 1533, Henry married **Anne Boleyn**. Archbishop Thomas Cranmer declared Henry's marriage to Catherine void and the marriage to Anne valid. Catherine was demoted to "Dowager Princess of Wales," and **Mary was declared illegitimate**. She was stripped of the title "Princess" and styled merely "The Lady Mary." Her household was dissolved, her servants dismissed, and she was sent to serve in her infant half-sister Elizabeth's household at Hatfield Palace. Mary **steadfastly refused** to acknowledge Anne as queen or Elizabeth as a princess, enraging Henry. The relationship between Mary and her father deteriorated so badly that they did not speak for three years. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Adolescence]

When Catherine of Aragon died in **January 1536**, Mary was "inconsolable." She was refused permission to visit her dying mother and grieved in semi-seclusion at Hunsdon in Hertfordshire. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Adolescence]

---

## Reconciliation with Henry VIII

In May 1536, Anne Boleyn was executed. Within two weeks, Henry married **Jane Seymour**, who urged reconciliation with Mary. Henry demanded that Mary recognize him as head of the Church of England, repudiate papal authority, and accept her own illegitimacy. After considerable resistance, Mary was **bullied into signing a document** agreeing to all of Henry's demands. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Adulthood]

Reconciled with her father, Mary returned to court and was granted her own household. Her principal residences included Hatfield House, the Palace of Beaulieu, Richmond, and Hunsdon. In **1537**, Jane Seymour died after giving birth to a son, **Edward**, who displaced Mary as heir apparent. Mary acted as godmother to Edward and chief mourner at Jane's funeral. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Adulthood]

In **1543**, Henry's sixth wife, **Catherine Parr**, helped bring the family closer together. Through the **Third Succession Act (1544)**, Henry restored Mary and Elizabeth to the line of succession after Edward — though both remained legally illegitimate. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Adulthood]

---

## Edward VI's Reign (1547–1553)

When Henry VIII died in **1547**, the nine-year-old Edward VI succeeded him. A regency council dominated by Protestants pushed through sweeping religious reforms, including the **Act of Uniformity 1549** and Cranmer's *Book of Common Prayer*. Mary **remained staunchly Catholic**, defiantly hearing traditional Mass in her own chapel. She appealed to her cousin Emperor Charles V to diplomatically pressure Edward's government to allow her to practice her religion. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Accession]

Religious tensions between Mary and Edward ran high. At Christmas **1550**, the 13-year-old Edward publicly upbraided the 34-year-old Mary for ignoring his worship laws, reducing both siblings to tears in front of the court. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Accession]

---

## Accession to the Throne (1553)

When Edward VI became terminally ill in 1553, he attempted to exclude both Mary and Elizabeth from the succession, naming instead his cousin **Lady Jane Grey** (granddaughter of Henry VIII's younger sister Mary) as his heir. Edward died on **6 July 1553** at age 15, likely of a lung infection (possibly tuberculosis). [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Accession]

Warned that a summons to London was a trap, Mary fled to **East Anglia**, where she owned extensive estates. On 9 July, from Kenninghall, Norfolk, she wrote to the Privy Council demanding she be proclaimed queen. She assembled a military force at **Framlingham Castle**, Suffolk. Support for the Protestant coup collapsed rapidly. Lady Jane was deposed on **19 July 1553**, and Mary rode triumphantly into London on **3 August 1553** accompanied by her half-sister Elizabeth and a procession of over 800 nobles and gentlemen. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Accession]

She was **crowned on 1 October 1553** at Westminster Abbey by Stephen Gardiner, Bishop of Winchester. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Reign]

---

## Reign (1553–1558)

### Religious Policy and the Marian Persecutions

One of Mary's first actions was to release imprisoned Catholic leaders, including the Duke of Norfolk and Stephen Gardiner. Her first Parliament declared her parents' marriage valid and abolished Edward's Protestant religious laws, restoring church doctrine to the form established in Henry VIII's **Six Articles of 1539**. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Religious policy]

Mary ultimately **reconciled England with Rome**, with Parliament repealing Henry's religious laws and returning the English church to papal jurisdiction — a major concession being that confiscated monastery lands were not returned to the Church but remained with their new owners. The **Heresy Acts were revived** in January 1555. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Religious policy]

The result was a campaign of religious persecution. The first burnings occurred over five days in **February 1555**: John Rogers on 4 February, Laurence Saunders on 8 February, and Rowland Taylor and John Hooper on 9 February. **Thomas Cranmer**, the former Archbishop of Canterbury, was forced to watch Bishops Nicholas Ridley and Hugh Latimer being burned; he initially recanted and rejoined Catholicism, but then dramatically withdrew his recantation on the day of his own burning. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Religious policy]

In total, **more than 280** religious dissenters were **burned at the stake** during Mary's reign. The burnings proved deeply unpopular — even Philip's own ecclesiastical adviser Alfonso de Castro condemned them, and ambassador Simon Renard warned that "cruel enforcement" could "cause a revolt." About 800 prominent Protestants, including John Foxe, fled into exile. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Religious policy]

### Marriage to Philip of Spain

At age 37, Mary sought a husband to produce an heir. Against general English sentiment that the queen should marry an Englishman, she married **Philip of Spain** (heir to the Habsburg Empire) on **25 July 1554**. The marriage was deeply unpopular, with many fearing England would become a mere province of the Habsburg Empire. The marriage treaty included safeguards for English interests, but opposition persisted. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Spanish marriage]

In **January 1556**, when Charles V abdicated, Philip became King of Spain and Mary became Queen consort of Spain. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Foreign policy]

### Phantom Pregnancies

Mary twice believed herself to be pregnant — once in **1554** and again in **1557–1558** — but no child was ever born. Modern historians speculate her symptoms may have been caused by **ovarian cysts or uterine cancer**. Her inability to produce an heir meant that the Protestant Elizabeth remained next in line to the throne. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Death]

### Foreign Policy and the Loss of Calais

Mary's marriage drew England into **Spain's war against France**. English forces won a victory at the **Battle of Saint Quentin** in August 1557, but in **January 1558**, the French besieged and captured **Calais** — England's last remaining possession on the European continent. The loss was a devastating blow to English pride and Mary's prestige. According to Holinshed's *Chronicles*, Mary lamented: **"When I am dead and opened, you shall find 'Calais' lying in my heart."** [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Foreign policy]

### Commerce and Economy

Mary's reign was plagued by **persistent rain and flooding** that led to famine. The Antwerp cloth trade declined, and England did not benefit from Spain's lucrative New World trade. On the positive side, Mary granted a royal charter to the **Muscovy Company** under Sebastian Cabot, and commissioned a world atlas from Diogo Homem. Her government also published a revised "Book of Rates" (1558) to reform tariff collection, and drafted plans for currency reform (though not implemented until after her death). [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Commerce and revenue]

---

## Death

Mary became weak and ill beginning in **May 1558**. She died on **17 November 1558**, aged 42, at **St James's Palace**, during an influenza epidemic that also claimed the life of Archbishop Reginald Pole later that same day. Philip, who was in Brussels, wrote to his sister: *"I felt a reasonable regret for her death."* [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Death]

Mary's funeral was held on 13 December 1558, and she was interred at **Westminster Abbey** on 14 December — not next to her mother as she had wished, but in a tomb she would eventually share with Elizabeth. The Latin inscription on their shared tomb, placed there by James I, reads: ***"Regno consortes et urna, hic obdormimus Elizabetha et Maria sorores, in spe resurrectionis"*** ("Consorts in realm and tomb, we sisters Elizabeth and Mary here lie down to sleep in hope of the resurrection"). [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Death]

She was succeeded by her half-sister, who became **Elizabeth I**.

---

## Legacy

At her funeral, Bishop John White of Winchester praised her: **"She was a king's daughter; she was a king's sister; she was a king's wife. She was a queen, and by the same title a king also."** [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Legacy]

Mary's legacy has been fiercely debated:

- **Protestant historians** long condemned her reign. By the 17th century, she had acquired the sobriquet **"Bloody Mary."** John Foxe's *Actes and Monuments* (1563), which vividly described the Marian persecutions, shaped enduring perceptions of Mary as a bloodthirsty tyrant and remained popular for centuries.
- **Catholic historians**, such as John Lingard, argued that Mary's policies failed not because they were wrong but because her reign was too short and plagued by natural disasters beyond her control.
- **Modern revisionist scholarship** since the 1980s has improved her reputation among academics. Christopher Haigh argued that her revival of religious festivities and Catholic practices was generally welcomed, and that "the last years of Mary's reign were not a gruesome preparation for Protestant victory, but a continuing consolidation of Catholic strength."
- Historian **Lucy Wooding** has noted misogynistic undertones in descriptions of Mary: "She's simultaneously being lambasted for being 'vindictive and fierce' and 'spineless and weak.'"

Although Mary's rule was ultimately ineffectual in many respects, the policies of **fiscal reform, naval expansion, and colonial exploration** later celebrated as Elizabethan accomplishments were in fact **started during Mary's reign**. [数据来源: Wikipedia – Mary I of England → Legacy]

---

## Key Dates Summary

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| 18 February 1516 | Born at Palace of Placentia, Greenwich |
| 1525 | Sent to preside over Council of Wales at Ludlow Castle |
| 1533 | Parents' marriage annulled; declared illegitimate |
| 1536 | Reconciled with Henry VIII after Anne Boleyn's execution |
| 1544 | Restored to line of succession (Third Succession Act) |
| 6 July 1553 | Edward VI dies |
| 19 July 1553 | Mary deposes Lady Jane Grey |
| 1 October 1553 | Crowned at Westminster Abbey |
| 25 July 1554 | Marries Philip of Spain |
| January 1555 | Heresy Acts revived; burnings begin |
| January 1556 | Becomes Queen consort of Spain |
| January 1558 | Loss of Calais to France |
| 17 November 1558 | Dies at St James's Palace, aged 42 |
